figures.caption: Table 4. Benchmarking: average number of classes for segmentation. Results on six standard benchmarks compared to all baselines, performing unsupervised video segmentation per video. While TW-FINCH remains a strong baseline, our method shows competitive results, comparing positively to ASOT (CVPR’24) and CLOT (ICCV’25) on 50 Salads and ABD (CVPR’22) on YTI.

Fig. 3. Model choices: (a) Kernel choice on 50 Salads [39]. The combination of the Gaussian kernel and NTK, as given by the Eq. (5), is the most accurate. (b) Input smoothing σ. We consider three datasets with varying video lengths 50 Salads[39] – long, Desktop Assembly [24] – medium, and YTI [40] – short. The choice of the smoothing hyperparameter s has effect, especially on long videos.

Table 5. Benchmarking: random number of classes for segmentation. Our proposed method repeatedly outperforms the TW-FINCH baseline in terms of F1 scores, when the ideal number of segments is not known, which is a realistic setting. We also show example predictions for the 5th video of each dataset.

Table 3. Hypothesis testing. (a) While TW-FINCH [2] can give precise boundaries when knowing the ground truth number of segments, it fails to do so for a random number of segments. (b) Two examples when predicting the true number of segments. (c) Tow examples when the desired number of segments is randomly varied. Our model can better predict repeated actions, and can better detect the boundaries.
